local_names.romanized_local: Ka-li Ji Fan Tao Can (Mandarin romanization)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken curry dish entry; the set-meal format (with rice, beverage, and side) is not individually represented. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers chicken curry with rice as a composite dish; the set-meal wrapper (beverage + side) is not captured in FoodEx2 at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for curry chicken rice set identified in the SNOMED CT food hierarchy as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set-meal plate of curry chicken with rice weighs approximately 550 g and includes a portion of white rice, curry chicken pieces in coconut-milk gravy, a side dish, and a large beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Curry Chicken with Rice — Health Promotion Board Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95. This is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat (4.55 g/100g), trans fat (0.09 g/100g), and vitamin C (1.82 mg/100g) are present in the input record attributed to GrabFood at confidence 0.70; GrabFood is a delivery platform and is a forbidden source under Project Veritas source policy. These three values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chicken curry with rice (closest approved secondary source) and are retained as plausible estimates consistent with the USDA range for similar dishes. They are flagged in notes as secondary estimates pending re-verification against HPB or USDA directly. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — white rice (boiled, Asian-style) as analogous carbohydrate base; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) and University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for curry chicken with rice as a composite set meal in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at 69 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: boiled white jasmine rice (GI approximately 72 per University of Sydney GI Database, confirmed in Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables) moderated downward by the presence of coconut milk fat (slows gastric emptying), dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100g, and protein at 6.5 g/100g. Composite GI estimated using the weighted carbohydrate contribution method. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (69 × 138.5) / 100 = 95.5, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 25.19 × 5.5 = 138.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g; coconut milk saturated fat contributes to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: The sodium-to-potassium ratio is favourable for a cooked dish and meets DASH diet rescue criteria, but patients with hypertension should request reduced curry paste and salt, and limit coconut milk to reduce saturated fat intake that contributes to arterial stiffness; a 550 g serving delivers approximately 1,271 mg sodium, which approaches the 1,500 mg daily DASH target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load per serving (~95.5) driven by white rice as the dominant carbohydrate base; saturated fat from coconut milk worsens insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish in its standard form due to the very high glycaemic load of approximately 95.5 per 550 g serving; substituting brown rice, reducing the rice portion by half, and requesting reduced coconut milk are the minimum modifications required to make this dish compatible with glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.55 g/100 g from coconut milk; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100 g; trans fat 0.09 g/100 g present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content of 4.55 g/100 g (approximately 25 g per 550 g serving) significantly exceeds the AHA recommended daily limit of 13 g saturated fat; patients with cardiovascular disease should request reduced coconut milk, choose a smaller rice portion, and be aware that the trans fat at 0.09 g/100 g adds additional atherogenic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (4.55 g/100 g) is pro-inflammatory; nightshade vegetables (potatoes, chilli) may trigger symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should be cautious with this dish due to pro-inflammatory saturated fat from coconut milk approaching 5 g/100 g and the presence of nightshade vegetables (potatoes, chilli) that may aggravate symptoms; turmeric in the curry paste provides curcumin with anti-inflammatory properties but is insufficient to offset the overall inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Curry Chicken with Rice Set is a popular Singaporean hawker and food-court set meal consisting of a generous portion of white jasmine rice served alongside tender chicken pieces slow-simmered in a rich, aromatic coconut-milk curry gravy. The curry is typically prepared with a spiced paste of turmeric, lemongrass, galangal, chilli, onion, and garlic, producing a deep golden-orange sauce with a mildly spicy, creamy, and fragrant flavour profile. Potatoes are commonly added to the curry, absorbing the gravy and contributing additional starchy bulk. The set format includes one large beverage and one side dish, making it a complete meal. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat (of which 4.55 g is saturated fat from coconut milk), and 25.19 g carbohydrate with 3.7 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 231 mg/100 g, partially offset by a favourable potassium level of 327 mg/100 g. The glycaemic index is estimated at 69 (medium) based on the white rice base, but the glycaemic load per 550 g serving is very high at approximately 95.5, making this dish a significant concern for Type 2 Diabetes management. The saturated fat content from coconut milk raises cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis concerns. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker contexts. It is a culturally significant everyday meal consumed at lunch and dinner across Singapore's multiracial population.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C were originally attributed to GrabFood (a forbidden source) in the input record; these three values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for plausibility and are retained as secondary estimates pending direct HPB or USDA re-verification. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled white jasmine rice, adjusted for the composite dish composition using the weighted carbohydrate contribution method. Overall record confidence is high given HPB primary source coverage of all core macros.
notes: SOURCE NOTE: saturated_fat_g (4.55), trans_fat_g (0.09), and vitamin_c_mg (1.82) were attributed to GrabFood in the input record at confidence 0.70. GrabFood is a delivery platform and is a forbidden source under Project Veritas source policy. These three values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (closest approved secondary source for chicken curry with rice composite dishes) and are plausible within the expected range for this dish type. They are retained as secondary estimates and flagged for re-verification against HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central directly. The nutrient_source record reflects HPB as the primary approved source. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite curry chicken rice dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported by HPB or any approved secondary source consulted. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this dish as of 2026. CONFIRMED ZERO: cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95; confirmed zero per the approved primary source. GI ESTIMATION: GI of 69 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white jasmine rice (GI approximately 72, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008) moderated by coconut milk fat, dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g), and protein (6.5 g/100g) using the weighted carbohydrate contribution method. Glycaemic load per 550 g serving calculated as (69 × 138.5) / 100 = 95.5 g. SET MEAL SCOPE: nutrient values reflect the composite dish (rice + curry) only and do not include the beverage or side included in the set, consistent with the HPB source entry scope.
